---
title: "ARIMA latent residuals: implementation notes"
description: "How epinowcast implements ARIMA(p, d, q) latent residuals on top of any module's linear predictor — the maths, the parameterisation, and the trade-offs."
author: Epinowcast Team
pkgdown:
  as_is: true
bibliography: library.bib
csl: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/citation-style-language/styles/master/apa-numeric-superscript-brackets.csl
link-citations: true
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{ARIMA latent residuals: implementation notes}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  eval = FALSE
)
```

# Introduction

This vignette describes how `epinowcast` implements ARIMA(p, d, q) latent residual terms on top of the linear predictor of any module that supports them.
The goal is to make the maths, the parameterisation, and the trade-offs explicit for anyone reading the Stan code or extending the implementation.
For usage examples and a comparison to `rw()` and lme4-style random effects, see the [latent processes vignette](latent-processes.html).
For the full model definition see the [model vignette](model.html).

# What an ARIMA term does

An `arima(time, by, p, d, q)` term in a formula declares a latent residual series that is added to the relevant linear predictor at every observation.
Writing the linear predictor of a module as $\eta_i$ for observation $i$ at time $t_i$ and group $g_i$:

$$\eta_i = X_i \beta + Z_i b + \epsilon_{t_i, g_i},$$

where $X_i \beta$ are the fixed effects, $Z_i b$ the random effects (including any `rw()` contributions, which are handled through the design matrix), and $\epsilon_{t_i, g_i}$ is the value of the ARIMA latent series for time $t_i$ and group $g_i$.
The residual evolves over the time axis declared in `arima(...)`; each observation just looks up the value at its own (time, group) index.

This is the same construction `brms` uses for `cor_arma`-style residual correlation on non-Gaussian likelihoods: a latent Gaussian residual added to the linear predictor, with ARMA structure imposed on its prior.

# The kernel decomposition

Let $T$ be the number of time points in the series and $G$ the number of groups.
The ARIMA latent is built from a $T \times G$ matrix of unit-normal shocks $z$ via

$$\epsilon = \sigma \, K(\phi, \theta, d) \, z,$$

where $\sigma$ is the latent standard deviation and $K$ is a $T \times T$ lower-triangular kernel that encodes the ARIMA structure.
The kernel factorises as

$$K(\phi, \theta, d) = D^d \, T(\psi),$$

where:

- $\psi \in \mathbb{R}^T$ is the truncated impulse response of the ARMA(p, q) process with autoregressive coefficients $\phi$ and moving-average coefficients $\theta$.
  It satisfies $\psi_1 = 1$ and the standard recursion

  $$\psi_k = \sum_{j=1}^{\min(p, k-1)} \phi_j \psi_{k-j} + \mathbb{1}\{k - 1 \le q\} \, \theta_{k-1}, \quad k = 2, \dots, T.$$

- $T(\psi) \in \mathbb{R}^{T \times T}$ is the lower-triangular Toeplitz matrix with first column $\psi$.
  Multiplying $T(\psi) z$ applies the ARMA filter to the shocks: each entry $(T(\psi) z)_t = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \psi_{t-k+1} z_k$.

- $D \in \mathbb{R}^{T \times T}$ is the lower-triangular cumulative-sum operator, with ones on and below the diagonal.
  $D^d$ integrates $d$ times.
  $D^0 = I$ leaves the ARMA series unchanged; $D^1$ produces an integrated series of order one; higher $d$ generalise.

Two properties of this factorisation are useful:

- **$D^d$ is data-only.** It can be precomputed and does not contribute to the autodiff graph in Stan.
- **$T(\psi)$ is parameter-dependent.** It is rebuilt every gradient evaluation, but it is built once per group (or once globally for `type = "dependent"`), then applied to all shocks in a single matrix multiplication.

Composing the two and applying once to shocks gives the full ARIMA(p, d, q) latent:

```text
psi  <- arma_impulse(phi, theta, T)        # impulse response, length T
T_K  <- lower_toeplitz(psi)                # T x T ARMA kernel
D_d  <- cumulative_op(T, d)                # T x T integration operator
K    <- D_d * T_K                          # T x T composed kernel
eps  <- sigma * K * z                      # T x G latent
```

The Stan implementation of these steps lives in `inst/stan/functions/arima_kernel.stan`.

# Stationarity via partial autocorrelations

A naive prior on $\phi$ does not enforce stationarity of the AR(p) process, and a non-stationary $\phi$ produces an impulse response that grows without bound, which destabilises sampling.
We avoid this by parameterising the AR coefficients through partial autocorrelations $r_1, \dots, r_p \in (-1, 1)$, which are unconstrained on this interval and map bijectively to stationary $\phi$ via the Durbin-Levinson recursion[@barndorff].

For $k = 1, \dots, p$, define $\phi_k^{(k)} = r_k$ and

$$\phi_j^{(k)} = \phi_j^{(k-1)} - r_k \phi_{k-j}^{(k-1)}, \quad j = 1, \dots, k-1.$$

The final coefficients $\phi_j = \phi_j^{(p)}$ are guaranteed to satisfy the stationarity condition (all roots of $1 - \phi_1 z - \dots - \phi_p z^p$ outside the unit circle).
This is the same approach `brms` and `rstanarm` use for AR(p) priors.

In Stan we declare `vector<lower=-1, upper=1>[p] expr_arima_pacf` directly, then transform to $\phi$ via `pacf_to_phi(pacf)` inside transformed parameters / model block.
The implicit uniform prior on each $r_k$ over $(-1, 1)$ is the default; users can override the bounds-induced prior via the priors mechanism if needed.

MA invertibility is not enforced explicitly.
For typical values it is rarely a sampling problem; if it becomes one for a specific use case, the same partial-autocorrelation transform applies symmetrically on $\theta$.

# Per-observation lookup

The kernel produces $\epsilon \in \mathbb{R}^{T \times G}$ but observations live in a flat vector indexed by $i = 1, \dots, n$.
Each observation $i$ knows its time index $t_i \in \{1, \dots, T\}$ and group index $g_i \in \{1, \dots, G\}$, both shipped from R as `time_idx` and `group_idx`.
The contribution to the linear predictor is added in a tight loop:

```stan
for (i in 1:expr_arima_n_obs) {
  r[i] += expr_arima_eps[expr_arima_time_idx[i], expr_arima_group_idx[i]];
}
```

The loop body is a single matrix lookup and an add; the loop length is bounded by the number of metadata rows in the relevant module (typically a few hundred), so the cost is negligible compared to the kernel construction.

# Group structure: dependent vs independent

`type = "dependent"` (the recommended default) shares $\phi$, $\theta$, and $\sigma$ across groups.
The kernel $K$ is built once and applied to each column of the $T \times G$ shock matrix in one matrix multiplication, with each group's series being driven by its own column of shocks.
This is appropriate when groups are believed to share dynamics (e.g. age strata of the same disease in the same country).

`type = "independent"` would fit per-group $\phi$, $\theta$, and $\sigma$.
The R-side metadata supports this distinction (the `expr_arima_type` data field), but the current Stan implementation builds a single shared kernel; switching to independent kernels per group is a follow-up that requires a small extension to the Stan code (loop over $g$ building per-group $K_g$).

# Special cases

The factorisation makes several familiar models special cases of the same code path:

- `arima(time, p = 0, d = 0, q = 0)` is degenerate and rejected by the constructor.
- `arima(time, p = 0, d = 1, q = 0)` reduces to a random walk: $\psi = (1, 0, 0, \dots)$, so $T(\psi) = I$ and $K = D$ is the cumulative-sum operator.
  This is mathematically equivalent to `rw(time)` (which uses the same cumulative-membership construction in fixed-effect form) and produces the same posterior up to parameterisation differences.
- `arima(time, p = 1, d = 0, q = 0)` is a stationary AR(1): $\psi_k = \phi^{k-1}$, $T(\psi)$ is the standard AR(1) Toeplitz, $K = T(\psi)$.
- `arima(time, p = 0, d = 1, q = 1)` is an integrated MA(1) (the random-walk-with-correlated-shocks model commonly seen in epidemiology).

The `arima_kernel_matrix()` function is unit-tested against these cases against `stats::ARMAtoMA` and direct R reference recursions; see `tests/testthat/test-stan_arima_kernel.R`.

# Priors

The default priors are deliberately weak and easy to override through the standard `priors` argument of the relevant module:

- $z_{t,g} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ — fixed by the non-centred parameterisation; not user-tunable.
- $\sigma \sim \mathcal{N}_+(0, 0.2)$ (half-normal) — controls how strongly the latent series can deviate from zero.
- $r_k \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(-1, 1)$ — implicit from the parameter bounds; weakly informative.
- $\theta_l \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ when MA terms are present — weakly informative.

The half-normal prior on $\sigma$ is the most consequential of these in practice; tighten it if the latent residual is competing with the rest of the linear predictor for variance.

# Where it is wired

The helper is wired into every module whose Stan-side predictor is indexed at the per-observation level:

- `enw_expectation()` — both the growth rate `r` formula and the latent-to-obs `observation` formula.
- `enw_reference()` non-parametric formula (`refnp`).
- `enw_missing()` formula (`miss`).

These modules all build their formula with `sparse = FALSE`, so the predictor returned by `combine_effects()` is one entry per observation, aligned with the per-observation lookup vectors that `arima()` produces.
The same Stan helper function `apply_arima_residual()` is used for all of them; the per-module Stan boilerplate is the parameter declarations and a single function call.

The reference parametric formulas (`refp_mean`, `refp_sd`) and the report formula (`rep`) build their formula with `sparse = TRUE`, returning a unique-row-indexed predictor that is mapped to observations later via a separate index vector.
Wiring `arima()` into those modules requires either switching them to `sparse = FALSE` when an ARIMA term is supplied, or applying the residual at the consumption site rather than the construction site.
Until that work lands, using `arima()` in those formulas will be parsed and shipped to Stan but the latent term will be inert there.

# Limitations

- One ARIMA term per formula. The R side aborts if multiple are supplied; lifting this requires the Stan side to gain a vector of latent series rather than a single one.
- AR stationarity is enforced; MA invertibility is not (rarely a problem in practice).
- `type = "independent"` is metadata-only; the Stan side currently uses shared dynamics regardless.
- The kernel is built dense ($T \times T$) every iteration. For very long series (thousands of time points) the autodiff cost grows as $O(T^2)$; in that regime a recursive form (computing $\epsilon$ via the ARMA filter directly) would be preferable.
- Reference parametric mean/sd and report-time formulas use a sparse (unique-row) predictor and are not yet wired; see "Where it is wired" above.

# References

1. Barndorff-Nielsen O, Schou G. On the parametrization of autoregressive models by partial autocorrelations. *Journal of Multivariate Analysis*, 1973.
2. Box GEP, Jenkins GM, Reinsel GC, Ljung GM. *Time Series Analysis: Forecasting and Control*. Wiley, 5th edition, 2015.
